preFix.title: Making a folio wallet
preFix.subtitle: A cloth-covered hard case for loose A4 or A5 sheets
preFix.excerpt: A folio wallet is two hard boards joined by a cloth-covered spine strip, opening flat to hold a stack of loose sheets, prints, or drawings. It closes to a clamshell-like form held by an elastic loop. This tutorial builds an A4 folio from bookbinding board, book cloth, and endpaper lining.
preFix.sourceNotes: The folio case and portfolio case structures are described in Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), Internet Archive, in the context of case-binding geometry and the construction of document folders. Arthur Johnson, The Thames and Hudson Manual of Bookbinding (1978) contains additional detail but is in copyright; structural description only is drawn here.

content.text: A
folio wallet
is a flat case with no sewn pages: two hard boards joined by a
spine strip

that opens flat, holds a stack of loose A4 sheets, drawings, or prints, and closes with an elastic loop. The structure is the same as a book case without the text block. Building one is a good introduction to board cutting, cloth covering, and the mitred
turn-in
corner. An A4 folio finished size is approximately 225 mm × 315 mm closed.

items.qty: 2 pieces, each 225 mm × 315 mm (adds 7.5 mm border around an A4 sheet)
items.name: Bookbinding board, 2 mm (2 pieces)

items.qty: one piece large enough to cover both boards and spine strip in one sheet: approx. 530 mm × 360 mm
items.name: Book cloth

items.qty: 2 pieces, each 218 mm × 308 mm (slightly smaller than each board face)
items.name: Endpaper stock (120–140 gsm) for inside lining

attrs.body: Sharp blades are safer than blunt ones: blunt blades skid. Change the blade at the first sign of dulling. Always cut on a self-healing cutting mat. Always cut against a metal ruler held firmly with the non-cutting hand behind the line of cut, never in front of it. Bookbinding adhesives: PVA is safe for indoor use without special ventilation. Clean brushes with warm water immediately after use.

content.text: Calculating the spine width

The spine strip width equals the maximum thickness of the sheets the folio will hold, plus 3-5 mm comfort allowance. For a stack of up to 50 sheets of 80 gsm copy paper (approximately 5 mm thick), a 10 mm spine strip is appropriate. For a portfolio holding prints on 200 gsm paper, measure the stack and add 5 mm.
Cut the spine strip from the same 2 mm board: dimensions are 10 mm wide (or your calculated width) × 315 mm tall. The
grain direction
on the spine strip must run parallel to the 315 mm height (parallel to the hinge), so the strip flexes easily when the folio is opened.
Covering in book cloth

The two boards and the spine strip are covered in one continuous piece of book cloth. This gives a neat hinge and avoids a visible seam at the spine. Cut the book cloth large enough to cover both boards and the spine strip placed side by side, with 20 mm clear on all four outer edges and a 3 mm gap between the inner edge of each board and the spine strip (to allow the hinge to flex without the cloth tearing).

Lay the book cloth face-down on a clean surface. Apply PVA to the back board first, centred on the left side of the cloth. Set the spine strip 3 mm to the right of the back board's right edge (the spine edge). Apply PVA to the spine strip and position it. Apply PVA to the front board and place it 3 mm to the right of the spine strip. The assembly should look like this: cloth, then 20 mm gap, then back board, then 3 mm gap, then spine strip, then 3 mm gap, then front board, then 20 mm gap, then cloth edge.

Flip the assembly over and rub down with the bone folder, starting from the centre of each board and working outward. Score the hinge lines on the cloth face with the bone folder against a ruler at the inner edges of the spine strip: one line at the gap between back board and spine strip, one at the gap between spine strip and front board. These score lines become the opening hinges of the folio.

Cut the mitres at all four outer corners of the cloth, leaving a small tongue of cloth at each corner tip. Fold the head and tail turn-ins first, using the bone folder to push the cloth firmly against the board edge. Fold the left and right turn-ins. Press the mitred corners flat. Leave under a flat weight overnight.
Lining the inside faces

Cut two pieces of endpaper stock to 218 mm × 308 mm: 7 mm smaller than the board face on each edge. Apply PVA thinly to the back face of one endpaper sheet. Place it centred on the inside face of one board, with equal margins on all four sides. Rub down with the bone folder through a piece of waste paper to avoid crushing the endpaper surface. Repeat for the second board. Leave under flat weights for at least an hour.
Adding the elastic closure

Pierce a hole with the bookbinding awl through the front board, centred at the foredge (the right edge when the folio is closed), 25 mm from the head edge of the board. Pierce a corresponding hole 25 mm from the tail edge, also at the foredge.

Thread the 4 mm elastic cord through the head hole from the outside of the board to the inside. Carry the cord down the inside face of the front board and out through the tail hole. Pull both ends of the cord even on the outside face, forming a loop. Tie the ends with a secure reef knot on the inside of the board, close to the holes. Trim the tails to 10 mm and dab with a small amount of PVA to prevent the knot slipping.

The elastic loop sits on the outside of the closed folio's foredge. To close the folio, stretch the loop over the left edge (the spine edge of the back board). The loop should hold the folio closed under light tension; a loop that sags needs a shorter cord.
Finishing

Fold the folio closed and press under a flat weight for an hour. The score lines at the hinge will ease the first few openings; after ten or twelve opens the hinge is fully broken in. A stiff new folio should not be forced open to a wide angle on the first use.
Care

Store the folio flat or upright. If stored upright, the spine strip takes the load; it is 2 mm board and will handle the weight of a moderate page stack without bowing. The book cloth surface can be wiped with a barely damp cloth. Do not leave the folio closed under a heavy weight for long periods with the elastic under tension; the cord may lose elasticity.
Variations
For an A3 folio, scale all measurements to 330 mm × 460 mm boards. A 3 mm bookbinding board is more appropriate at A3 size; 2 mm board at A3 can flex when a heavy stack is inside.

A folio wallet with a leather exterior uses the same board construction. Replace the book cloth with a 1.0-1.5 mm vegetable-tanned leather, pasted with PVA diluted to the consistency of thick cream. Leather turn-ins need skiving at the edges to reduce the bulk at the corners.

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
